entries.text: Okay, I know this is going to ruffle
some feathers, but living in Korea as a
foreigner sometimes can feel extremely
overrated. If you're watching this from
abroad, scrolling through Tik Toks of
cherry blossoms and K-pop concerts
thinking, "Oh my god, I wish that was my
life." Then this is the video for you.
Because the truth is, and I'm going to
hold your hand while I tell you this,
the career that is trending on social
media right now and the career that you
live in your day-to-day life are not the
same thing. Hi everyone, I'm G. I'm a
British foreigner who has been living in
Korea for a couple of years now. I love
a lot of things about my life here. I
love the safety, the food, and the
convenience of living in Seoul. But I
also think that it is very important to
talk about things that aren't really
shown online. So today I really want to
break down is living in Korea overrated
as a foreigner and are people just
chasing a version of Korea that never
really existed? So let's start off by
talking about what most people see
online which is of course the Instagram
and Tik Tok Korea vibe that is often
very overromanticized.
All the gorgeous cafes. Absolutely
flawless skincare routines, aka the
glass skin routine that went extremely
viral online. Got people living in
apartments that look like they came
straight off of a Pinterest board
filming 30-second clips of themselves in
said Pinterestesque
apartments with perfectly glowing skin
in their seemingly perfect lives. Add in
K-pop, Kdrama, Korean skincare, Korean
beauty standards, and suddenly Korea
becomes more than just a country. It
becomes an aesthetic goal. But here's
the thing, that version of Korea is
curated. That is a highlight reel and it
sets people up for some serious culture
shock when they actually move here. The
Korea fantasy sells hard. The idea that
life here is just easier, that everyone
you meet is incredibly stylish and
incredibly attractive, that you'll make
cute friends, fall in love with a
Kdramaesque looking person, and suddenly
you'll just have perfect glowing skin,
and have this amazing life that has been
shown to you online. But here's where it
flips. People start chasing that fantasy
as a solution to the problems that they
are facing back in their home country.
They come to Korea expecting to find
some healing, some sense of belonging, a
sense of personal reinvention, if you
will. And to be fair, there are a lot of
things in Korea that do make your life
easier. As a Brit, I have to say I love
that Korea is for the most part very
clean. To me, the public transportation
in Korea is just absolute god tier. I'm
used to the British transportation
systems. And if you know how bad those
are, then you'll know why I appreciate
Korea's transportation so much. Also,
you can just pop out to a convenience
store at like 2:00 in the morning and
not be scared for your life. But the
issue is that people's expectations of
Korea are so much higher than just
having, you know, good public
transportation. Due to this perfect
image image image Due to this perfect
image of Korea that has been circulating
online, people have grown to have
extremely high expectations for Korea.
And when Korea doesn't meet those
people's expectations when they move
here, they come crashing down to earth
and they come crashing down hard. Here's
where things start to get a bit real.
Living in Korea as a foreigner is not
the same as being a tourist. When you
actually live here, you have to deal
with the stress of visas,
right? Much bear knows all about the
stress of visas. Apparently, you have to
deal with all of the paperwork, the
banking hurdles. That's a huge one
because some of the experiences I've had
at banks in Korea have not been the
best. Job market for foreigners is
a no bueno. The job market one, I think,
is something that not enough foreigners
really consider before moving to Korea
because the most common job for
foreigners here is English teaching. And
unless you come with the EPIC program,
the English program in Korea, then I
really do not recommend trying to teach
in Korea. Hogwans. I would not come
within 10 feet of any of them. I have a
whole video on why I quit my teaching
job here and that goes more into depth
on the whole teaching thing. I don't
want to get too off track of this video.
So, if you want to watch that, I will
link it below. Long story short, don't
teach at Hogwarts. So, with all that
being said, you realize in Korea very
quickly that you don't always fit here.
You might learn Korean and speak Korean
fluently. You might learn everything
there is to know about Korean culture
and respect the culture, but at the end
of the day, you will always be a
foreigner here. You will always be seen
as an outsider. There have been
countless occasions where I have been
out in public with my Korean husband and
they will always address him. They will
not look at me. They always look at him
and speak directly to him. I sometimes
have had full-blown conversations
through my husband, via my husband to
the person who is speaking to us. They
don't make eye contact with me. They
don't address me directly. It all goes
through my husband. I'll be on the side
like no. Hi. In fact, in my experience
as a foreigner here, just your mere
presence can make people feel
uncomfortable. here. I was thinking I

oh thank god I don't have to speak in
English but this feeds in to my next
point which I feel very rarely gets

toll overall that living in Korea can
have on you. You can be surrounded by
people every single day and yet still
feel incredibly alone. And I think a lot
of foreigners in Korea struggle with
this silent, low-level isolation.
Especially when I first moved to Korea
and my Korean language skills were very,
very bad. I wouldn't really understand
most of what was being said in
conversations around me. When I used to
go out for company dinners with my
co-workers, I rarely felt like I was
included in conversations. You get tired
of kind of having to explain yourself.
And I think over time you begin to
wonder if anyone actually knows the real
you anymore. It's also exhausting in my
experience to constantly perform. You
know, you don't want to offend anybody
and you're trying to be really
respectful all the time. You're
navigating these really complex social
situations with very limited language
skills. Granted, my Korean language
skills are better now than they were
back then, but I'm still not completely
fluent in Korean. One day, I will be
fluent in Korean, but I digress. I
remember in my first year this feeling
of like wanting to stop standing out so
much. I wanted people to stop staring at
me and I wanted to kind of just blend in
with my environment better, which if
you're a foreigner, that is impossible
[laughter]
in Korea. And I found myself often
wanting to just dim myself down as much
as possible so that I would stand out
less. So, you kind of start to become
this very filtered version of yourself.
And I remember seeing a real on

I can find it, but honestly, I doubt
I'll be able to.
I'm a long-distance daughter, but I

>> There's this immense guilt that comes
with being a long-distance child. I
think some people who have this fantasy
of career in their head don't really

living in Korea as a foreigner

Korea is the fantasy that people sell

this perfect person with perfect skin
and that suddenly your life is going to

That's overrated. But the real career,
the career where you go hiking in
mountains and have old people outpacing

constantly inflate your ego by telling
you how beautiful you are.
the career that I was immediately
welcomed into with kindness on my first
ever day by the beautiful strangers in
the airport who helped me navigate the

heavy suitcases. That's the career that
is absolutely priceless and could never
be overrated to me. I've gone through
phases here. I've had the falling in

is the best place on earth phase. I've
been through the having a horrendous
hogwan job and suddenly hating
everything about Korea phase and now

life in Korea and started building my
own life in Korea. And that shift that
shift is what's made it worth staying
for me. If you're watching this and
you're feeling disillusioned and
homesick, believe me, I've been there.
You're not alone. It's okay to grieve
the fantasy of Korea that you had in

while life in Korea is amazing in so
many ways, it's not perfect, and it
definitely isn't for everyone. But what
do you think? Do you think Korea is
